question: What drew you to Geography, and when did you realise it was what you wanted to study?
lookingFor: A genuine, specific moment or thread of curiosity — not a generic 'I like the world' answer.

bullets: Name a specific experience, topic, or moment that lit the spark
Show how that interest deepened over time into a decision
Connect it to what you want to get out of studying the subject at degree level
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It started with a family trip to Iceland when I was fifteen — I remember standing on the rift between the North American and Eurasian plates and genuinely feeling disoriented that I was watching geology happen in real time. That sent me down a rabbit hole of physical geography, but then a module on migration and urban inequality in sixth form pulled me toward the human side too. I realised Geography was the one subject that kept both threads alive, and that the interplay between the physical world and the people living in it is exactly where I want to spend my time.

commonMistakes: Vague answers like 'I've always loved maps' with no substance
Describing a school trip without linking it to deeper intellectual interest
Focusing only on one branch of geography when the discipline spans both physical and human
followUps: Has your interest shifted more toward physical or human geography as you've learned more?

question: Why study Geography at university rather than a more specialised degree in, say, environmental science or economics?
lookingFor: Awareness of what a Geography degree uniquely offers — its interdisciplinary breadth — and a genuine reason why that suits the applicant.

bullets: Articulate what Geography offers that narrower degrees don't
Tie that breadth to a personal intellectual interest or career direction
Show you've thought about the degree, not just the subject name
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Geography is the only degree that let me walk into a room studying glacial retreat in the morning and housing inequality in the afternoon, and I genuinely need both. I could have done environmental science, but I kept being drawn back to the human dimensions — why some communities are exposed to risk while others aren't, and how policy shapes that. Geography gives me the tools to hold those questions together properly. It also tends to produce graduates who can translate between disciplines, and that's a skill I think the problems I care about actually need.

commonMistakes: Saying you chose Geography because you weren't sure what else to do
Dismissing adjacent disciplines rather than explaining your positive reason for Geography
No engagement with what a Geography degree actually covers
followUps: Are there aspects of the Geography degree you're less interested in — and how will you approach them?

question: What specifically attracted you to our Geography department rather than others?
lookingFor: Evidence that the applicant has done real research — named modules, staff, fieldwork, or department strengths — not generic praise.

bullets: Name something specific: a module, a professor's research, a fieldwork destination, a specialism
Link it to your own interests or goals
Show you understand what makes this department's approach distinctive
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Your department's emphasis on combining quantitative GIS methods with qualitative fieldwork really caught my attention — a lot of programmes feel like they're one or the other. I read some of Dr. Marsh's work on urban heat islands and informal settlements, and that intersection of climate and social vulnerability is exactly what I want to explore. I also noticed the dissertation fieldwork option in sub-Saharan Africa, which matters to me because I want to develop skills in a context that isn't just Western Europe. The combination of those things is why your programme stood out.

commonMistakes: Generic praise that would fit any university's geography department
Mentioning only location or league table position
Naming a professor whose work is unrelated to your stated interests
followUps: How do you see your interests developing over the three or four years of the course?

question: Tell us about a book, article, or documentary you've engaged with recently that's shaped how you think about Geography.
lookingFor: Evidence of independent intellectual engagement beyond the syllabus — and the ability to discuss ideas critically, not just summarise.

bullets: Name a specific text or source and summarise the core argument clearly
Engage critically — what did it challenge, confirm, or complicate for you?
Connect it to a broader geographical idea or to your own interests
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I read Siddharth Kara's 'Cobalt Red' earlier this year and it fundamentally changed how I think about commodity chains. It's ostensibly about mining in the DRC, but it's really about how the geography of extraction maps almost perfectly onto historical patterns of colonialism and political marginalisation. What struck me was that the supply chain is geographically invisible to end consumers — the phone in your pocket has no coordinates on it. That got me thinking seriously about how geography can be deliberately obscured, and how making it visible is actually a political act.

commonMistakes: Summarising the book without offering any personal or critical response
Choosing a GCSE textbook as 'wider reading'
Picking something so general it shows no real engagement with Geography as a discipline
followUps: Did anything in it make you question something you'd previously taken for granted?

question: Can you explain why earthquakes and volcanoes tend to cluster in specific parts of the world?
lookingFor: A clear, accurate explanation of plate tectonics and plate boundaries — with some sense of why the distribution matters geographically.

bullets: Explain the concept of tectonic plates and boundary types
Link boundary types to earthquake and volcanic activity clearly
Mention a real example or two to ground the explanation
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The Earth's crust is broken into large plates that float on the mantle and move relative to each other — usually only a few centimetres a year. Where those plates meet, you get intense geological activity. At destructive boundaries, where one plate subducts under another, you get both deep earthquakes and volcanic arcs — like the Andes or the Japanese island chain. At constructive boundaries, like the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, plates pull apart and magma wells up. Transform boundaries, like the San Andreas Fault, produce powerful earthquakes but not usually volcanoes. The Ring of Fire around the Pacific concentrates so much activity because the Pacific Plate is being subducted on multiple sides simultaneously.

commonMistakes: Confusing boundary types or misattributing which causes which activity
Giving a textbook recitation with no examples
Not explaining why the distribution is geographically significant for human populations
followUps: Why do some densely populated areas sit directly on these boundaries, and what does that mean for risk management?

question: What drives urbanisation, and why is it happening most rapidly in the Global South?
lookingFor: Understanding of the push-pull factors behind urbanisation and awareness of the global patterns and their underlying causes.

bullets: Explain push factors from rural areas and pull factors toward cities
Account for why rates are higher in the Global South than in already-urbanised nations
Show some awareness of the consequences — both opportunities and challenges
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Urbanisation happens when more people move to cities than leave them, driven by a mix of push and pull factors. Rural agricultural decline — mechanisation, land consolidation, drought — pushes people out, while the promise of employment, services, and social mobility pulls them toward cities. The Global South is urbanising fastest because many of those countries are going through structural economic changes that Europe and North America went through in the 19th century, compressed into a much shorter timeframe and without the same industrial base to absorb the inflow. The result is rapid growth that formal housing and infrastructure can't keep pace with, which is why we see such large informal settlement populations in cities like Lagos, Mumbai, or Nairobi.

commonMistakes: Treating urbanisation as uniformly positive or uniformly negative
Giving only economic factors and ignoring social, political, or environmental drivers
Describing the Global South as a homogeneous bloc
followUps: Is rapid urbanisation a problem to be managed or an opportunity to be harnessed — what's your view?

question: How does climate change interact with existing inequalities in Geography — who is most affected, and why?
lookingFor: Ability to connect physical processes to human geography — and to reason about spatial inequality rather than just recite climate facts.

bullets: Show that exposure to climate hazards is not random — it maps onto existing patterns of inequality
Give concrete examples of differential vulnerability (e.g. low-lying nations, subsistence farmers)
Reflect on why the communities least responsible often bear the most risk
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Climate change amplifies existing inequalities rather than creating entirely new ones. The communities most exposed — low-lying Pacific Island states, subsistence farmers in the Sahel, coastal slum dwellers in Bangladesh — tend to have contributed least to cumulative emissions but have the fewest resources to adapt. There's a cruel geography to it: the countries in tropical and subtropical bands are warming faster and experiencing more extreme weather events, while the industrialised nations that built their prosperity on fossil fuels sit at latitudes that, at least for now, face less severe immediate effects. It's not just about physical exposure either — adaptive capacity matters enormously. A farmer in Iowa facing a drought has insurance, credit, and a government safety net. A smallholder in Mozambique facing the same drought has almost none of those buffers.

commonMistakes: Treating climate change as a purely physical or environmental topic with no human geography dimension
Only naming rich nations as impacted — missing the differential vulnerability point entirely
Conflating global warming with uniform warming everywhere
followUps: What's your view on the concept of 'climate justice' — is it a useful frame or does it complicate the policy response?

question: What is GIS, and how has it changed how geographers do their work?
lookingFor: A clear explanation of what GIS is, with some genuine sense of how spatial analysis has transformed geographical inquiry.

bullets: Define GIS accurately and explain what it can do that maps alone cannot
Give an example of a geographical question GIS helps answer
Reflect on any limitation or critique of the tool
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: GIS — Geographic Information Systems — is a way of layering and analysing spatially referenced data. Unlike a static map, GIS lets you ask questions of the data: find all the areas within 500 metres of a river that also have soil types prone to erosion and a population density above a certain threshold. That kind of spatial query would have taken weeks with paper overlays and now takes seconds. It's transformed fieldwork too — researchers can link GPS data collected on the ground with satellite imagery and census data in one environment. The limitation I find interesting is that GIS can look very authoritative and objective, but the data going in, and the decisions about how to classify and visualise it, are all made by people with assumptions. So it's a powerful tool that also requires critical literacy.

commonMistakes: Describing GIS as just 'digital mapping' — missing the analytical dimension
No concrete example of what it's actually used for
Treating GIS as infallible or not engaging with its limitations
followUps: Have you used any GIS software yourself, even at a basic level?

question: Why do borders matter so much in human geography, and can you give an example where a border has had a profound effect on people's lives?
lookingFor: Conceptual understanding of borders as political constructions with real human consequences — not just lines on a map.

bullets: Explain that borders are social and political constructions, not natural features
Give a specific, well-chosen example with real human consequences
Reflect on what that tells us about power and space
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Borders are among the most consequential geographical features in human life, but they're not natural — they're political agreements imposed on landscapes, and often on communities that predate them. The example I keep coming back to is the Partition of British India in 1947. The Radcliffe Line was drawn in weeks, with limited knowledge of the territory, and it severed communities, religious sites, farmland, and rail networks that had functioned together for generations. The resulting mass migration — perhaps 14 to 17 million people — was one of the largest in history. What it illustrates is how a line on a map doesn't just divide space, it divides identities, resources, and access. The border became a machine for producing displacement.

commonMistakes: Treating borders as natural or neutral features of the landscape
Choosing an example but failing to explain why it matters geographically
Only discussing contemporary examples without any historical depth
followUps: Are there contexts where open or porous borders have worked well — and what does that suggest?

question: How do glaciers shape landscapes, and what evidence can you read in a post-glacial environment?
lookingFor: Sound understanding of glacial erosion and deposition processes, with the ability to link processes to recognisable landforms.

bullets: Describe the main erosional processes — abrasion and plucking
Name key landforms produced and explain briefly how they form
Show awareness that we can read a landscape's glacial history from its features today
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Glaciers erode through two main processes: abrasion, where rock fragments embedded in the ice act like sandpaper and grind down the bedrock; and plucking, where the glacier freezes onto jointed rock and rips chunks away. Together these produce characteristic landforms — U-shaped valleys carved out by the glacier's bulk, corries or cirques where the glacier first formed and rotational slip deepened the hollow, arêtes where two corries eroded back-to-back, and roche moutonnées where differential erosion leaves smooth upstream faces and jagged downstream ones. When the ice retreated it also deposited material — drumlins, moraines, erratics carried far from their source. Walking through a Scottish glen, for example, you can read the whole glacial story in the valley shape, the hanging valleys where tributaries were left high above the main trunk, and the boulders that don't match the local geology.

commonMistakes: Confusing erosional and depositional landforms
Describing processes without linking them to the landforms they produce
Treating glaciation as purely historical — missing that active glaciers still shape environments today
followUps: How is accelerating glacial retreat changing the landscapes we associate with glaciation?

question: What is the demographic transition model, and what are its limits as a tool for understanding population change?
lookingFor: Accurate knowledge of the model alongside genuine critical thinking about its assumptions and applicability.

bullets: Describe the four (or five) stages of the model accurately
Explain the logic behind each transition — why birth and death rates change
Offer a substantive critique: Eurocentric assumptions, compressed transitions, or stages not experienced uniformly
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The demographic transition model describes how societies move from high birth and death rates in Stage 1, through falling death rates as sanitation and healthcare improve in Stage 2, to falling birth rates as education, urbanisation, and female empowerment take hold in Stage 3, reaching low birth and death rates in Stage 4 — and some countries have added a Stage 5 with sub-replacement fertility. The model is elegant because it captures a real pattern, but its limits matter. It was built on the historical experience of Western Europe, where the transition happened gradually over 200 years. Many countries in the Global South are moving through it far more rapidly, under completely different conditions — aid dependency, structural adjustment, very young age structures — so the mechanisms aren't always the same. It also treats 'development' as linear and universal, which elides huge variation within countries and regions. And it says nothing about migration, which is now one of the dominant drivers of population geography in many places.

commonMistakes: Describing the stages without any critical evaluation of the model
Getting the sequence of birth and death rate changes wrong
Treating the model as a prediction rather than a descriptive generalisation
followUps: Can you think of a country whose population story doesn't fit neatly into the model?

question: How does a river change in character from its source to its mouth, and why?
lookingFor: Solid understanding of the long profile of a river and how discharge, gradient, and sediment load interact to produce different channel characteristics.

bullets: Describe the upper, middle, and lower course characteristics clearly
Explain the processes — erosion dominant upstream, deposition dominant downstream
Link to recognisable landforms at each stage
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In its upper course a river typically has a steep gradient but low discharge, so it cuts vertically rather than laterally — you get V-shaped valleys, interlocking spurs, and waterfalls where the river hits resistant rock. As it picks up tributaries and its discharge grows, the gradient flattens out in the middle course and the river starts to swing sideways, eroding on the outside of bends and depositing on the inside to form meanders. In the lower course, discharge is high but the gradient is very gentle — the river is moving slowly and can no longer carry much sediment, so it deposits it, building a floodplain, levées, and eventually a delta or estuary at the coast. The whole system is essentially a machine for moving material from high ground to the sea, and the character at any point reflects where the river is in that journey.

commonMistakes: Confusing which processes dominate at which stage — vertical erosion isn't limited to the upper course in all cases
Describing landforms without explaining the processes that create them
Ignoring the role of discharge in driving downstream changes
followUps: How does human modification — dams, channelisation — disrupt those natural processes?

question: What are the limitations of using GDP per capita as a measure of development?
lookingFor: Critical thinking about measurement — understanding that GDP captures economic output but misses distribution, wellbeing, and non-market activity.

bullets: Explain what GDP per capita actually measures
Identify at least two substantive limitations
Name an alternative or complementary measure and why it improves on GDP
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: GDP per capita tells you the average economic output per person in a country, but 'average' is doing a lot of work there. A country can have a high GDP per capita with enormous inequality — the wealth is real but it's concentrated in a small group and most people's lives don't reflect the average. It also counts all economic activity equally: cleaning up an oil spill adds to GDP, even though it's responding to damage. And it completely ignores unpaid work — the care work and subsistence farming that are the economic backbone of many communities, particularly women's labour. That's why measures like the Human Development Index, which adds life expectancy and education alongside income, or the Gini coefficient for inequality, or Bhutan's Gross National Happiness framework, are more geographically interesting — they start to reveal the human texture behind the number.

commonMistakes: Only naming one limitation superficially
Not being able to suggest what a better measure might look like
Conflating GDP with GDP per capita
followUps: If you were designing a development index, what three indicators would you include and why?

question: Why are some coastlines eroding rapidly while others are stable or even accreting?
lookingFor: Understanding of the balance between erosional and depositional processes, and the role of rock type, fetch, and human intervention.

bullets: Identify the key factors that determine erosion rates — wave energy, geology, fetch
Explain why deposition can lead to accretion in some contexts
Acknowledge the role of human management in disrupting natural sediment systems
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Coastal erosion rate is basically a product of wave energy meeting rock resistance. Where you have a long fetch — open ocean stretching thousands of kilometres — and relatively weak or heavily jointed rock, like the chalk cliffs of Holderness on the Yorkshire coast, you get very rapid erosion — Holderness loses around two metres a year. Harder, more resistant geology like granite in Cornwall erodes far more slowly. Deposition happens where wave energy is lower and longshore drift brings sediment to an embayment — you get beaches building and, in sheltered areas, salt marshes and mudflats that can actually extend the coastline seaward. The complicating factor today is that human engineering — sea walls, groynes, dredging — often interrupts the natural sediment budget. Groynes trap sediment on one side but starve the beach downdrift, which can actually accelerate erosion further along the coast.

commonMistakes: Treating erosion as purely a function of wave power without mentioning geology
Ignoring human modification of coastal processes
Not explaining the concept of sediment budget or longshore drift
followUps: How should coastal communities respond to erosion — hard engineering, managed retreat, or something else?

question: Tell us about a piece of fieldwork or independent investigation you've carried out — what did you find, and what would you do differently?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine data collection, methodological thinking, and the self-awareness to reflect critically on limitations.

bullets: Describe the enquiry question and methodology clearly and concisely
State a finding and what it suggested
Offer a genuine methodological critique — not just 'we should have done more samples'
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I investigated whether river channel characteristics change predictably downstream along a local stream for my A-level coursework. I measured width, depth, velocity, and bedload particle size at six sites from the upper to lower course. The results broadly supported Bradshaw's model — discharge increased and particle size decreased downstream — but velocity didn't follow the expected pattern at site four, which sat just below a confluence that had been heavily culverted. That deviation was actually the most interesting finding: the human modification was distorting the natural pattern. Looking back, I'd have mapped the full history of engineering works on that stream first, so I could have controlled for that variable better rather than treating it as an anomaly at the end.

commonMistakes: Describing what you did without mentioning what you found
A 'critique' that's really just 'we needed more time or data'
No sense of what the findings actually mean geographically
followUps: What question would you want to investigate next if you could extend the project?

question: Is globalisation a force for convergence — making places more alike — or does it reinforce difference?
lookingFor: Nuanced thinking about a contested geographical concept — not a binary answer but engagement with the tension between the two.

bullets: Acknowledge that globalisation does produce some convergence — brands, architectures, consumption patterns
But argue or explore how it also produces and reinforces geographical difference
Use a specific example to anchor the argument
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The convergence narrative is real at a superficial level — you can find a Starbucks in the same glass tower design in Tokyo, Dubai, and São Paulo. But I think the more interesting geographical story is how globalisation also deepens differences. It concentrates particular industries in particular places: financial services in a handful of cities, manufacturing in specific labour-cost corridors, commodity extraction in regions that remain economically peripheral. So globalisation doesn't flatten place — it reassigns roles within a hierarchy. Cities like Shenzhen have been radically transformed by global capital while rural hinterlands in the same country remain largely outside that circuit. The concept I find useful is 'uneven development' — globalisation is a process that works through places differentially, and that selectivity is the point, not a side effect.

commonMistakes: Arguing simply one way without acknowledging the other side
Describing globalisation with no specific geographical examples
Treating globalisation as a recent phenomenon rather than something with a long historical geography
followUps: Does the recent trend toward deglobalisation — supply chain reshoring, trade barriers — change your analysis?

question: Why does the UK experience such variable and unpredictable weather?
lookingFor: Understanding of the UK's mid-latitude position, the influence of air masses, and the role of the jet stream and depressions.

bullets: Explain the UK's position at the meeting point of contrasting air masses
Describe how mid-latitude depressions and the jet stream drive variability
Show awareness that 'unpredictable' doesn't mean random — there are patterns
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The UK sits at a mid-latitude position where several air masses converge: cold, dry polar continental air from the northeast, warm moist tropical maritime air from the southwest, and cold wet polar maritime air from the northwest. Whichever one dominates on a given day largely determines the character of the weather — and they jostle for control constantly. The jet stream, a high-altitude band of fast-moving air, guides the track of Atlantic depressions across or around the UK, and the position of the jet stream shifts seasonally and year to year, which is why one summer can be hot and dry and another relentlessly wet. Fronts — the boundaries between different air masses — bring rapid and sometimes dramatic weather changes as they pass through. It's not random, but it's a complex enough system that forecasting more than a few days out remains genuinely difficult.

commonMistakes: Attributing UK weather only to the Gulf Stream, which is one factor among many
No mention of air masses or frontal systems
Describing individual weather events without explaining the underlying atmospheric dynamics
followUps: How is climate change affecting the jet stream, and what might that mean for UK weather patterns?

question: What do geographers mean by 'sense of place', and why does it matter?
lookingFor: Engagement with the human geography concept of place as socially constructed and emotionally meaningful — not just a location.

bullets: Distinguish place from space — place has meaning, identity, and attachment
Explain that sense of place is constructed and contested, not fixed
Give an example that shows why this matters practically or politically
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Geographers distinguish between 'space' — which is abstract and geometric — and 'place', which is space invested with meaning through human experience and memory. Sense of place is the emotional and cultural attachment that people develop to particular locations, and it's deeply personal but also shaped by shared history and culture. Geographers like Doreen Massey argued that place should be understood as relational and open — always connected to elsewhere through flows of people, capital, and ideas — rather than as a bounded, fixed essence. Why it matters becomes very concrete when you look at how strongly people defend their sense of place against what they experience as threats to it: regeneration schemes that displace long-term residents, or industrial development in landscapes held to be culturally significant. The resistance often gets dismissed as NIMBYism, but it's geographically richer than that — it's about who has the power to define what a place is and who it's for.

commonMistakes: Treating sense of place as purely emotional and ignoring the social and political dimensions
Not distinguishing between place and space
Treating sense of place as fixed and uncontested
followUps: Can you think of an example where two groups have competing senses of the same place?

question: Why are the human impacts of natural hazards so unequal between wealthy and less wealthy countries?
lookingFor: Clear thinking about the distinction between a hazard and a disaster, and how economic and social factors shape vulnerability and resilience.

bullets: Distinguish between a natural hazard and a disaster — it's the intersection with human vulnerability that matters
Explain the factors that create differential vulnerability: building standards, early warning, healthcare, insurance
Use a comparative example to make the argument concrete
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The key insight is that a natural hazard only becomes a disaster when it meets a vulnerable population — and vulnerability is socially and economically produced, not geographically inevitable. The 2010 Haiti earthquake and the 2011 Christchurch, New Zealand earthquake were of comparable magnitude, but Haiti lost over 200,000 people and New Zealand lost 185. The difference wasn't the earthquake — it was building standards, emergency response capacity, healthcare systems, and the ability of communities to recover economically afterwards. Wealthier countries can invest in early warning systems, enforce seismic-safe construction codes, and mobilise rapid relief. Less wealthy countries often can't, and debt and aid dependency can limit the recovery phase as much as the event itself. The hazard is physical; the disaster is political and economic.

commonMistakes: Attributing differential impacts purely to the severity of the physical event
Implying poorer countries experience more hazards, rather than being more vulnerable to the same hazards
No concrete comparative example to anchor the argument
followUps: What policies could reduce vulnerability in the most exposed communities — and what stands in the way?

question: What distinguishes a refugee from an economic migrant, and why does the distinction matter so much politically?
lookingFor: Accurate knowledge of the legal definitions alongside critical awareness of why the boundary is contested and politically loaded.

bullets: Give the legal definition of a refugee under the 1951 Refugee Convention
Explain what characterises economic migration
Reflect on why the distinction is contested and what the political stakes are
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Under the 1951 Refugee Convention, a refugee is someone who has fled their country because of a well-founded fear of persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership of a particular social group. An economic migrant, by contrast, moves primarily in search of better living standards or employment opportunities — a choice rather than a compulsion, legally speaking. The distinction matters enormously because it determines who has a legal right to protection and who can be deported. But the political intensity around it comes partly from how blurry the boundary is in practice. Someone fleeing extreme poverty caused by climate change, or structural violence in a country without an active war, may have a compelling claim on our solidarity without neatly meeting the Convention's criteria. States tend to narrow the refugee definition when migratory pressure is high, which has a direct bearing on asylum approval rates. Geographers and legal scholars increasingly argue the categories are inadequate for the complexity of contemporary displacement.

commonMistakes: Not knowing the 1951 Convention definition
Treating the distinction as straightforward and uncontested
Missing the political economy of why states have an interest in classifying people one way or the other
followUps: How does climate displacement challenge the existing legal framework for protecting migrants?

question: What is 'resource curse', and why doesn't the discovery of oil or minerals automatically lead to development?
lookingFor: Understanding of the paradox of plenty — and the economic and political mechanisms that explain it.

bullets: Define the resource curse and name the concept accurately
Explain the mechanisms: Dutch Disease, corruption, conflict, neglect of other sectors
Give an example of a country that exhibits the pattern — and ideally one that has avoided it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The resource curse is the paradox whereby countries with abundant natural resources often have lower economic growth and worse development outcomes than countries with fewer resources. Several mechanisms drive it. 'Dutch Disease' describes how commodity export revenues cause a country's currency to appreciate, making its manufactured goods uncompetitive internationally and hollowing out the broader economy. Reliance on resource rents can also undermine state-building: governments that don't need tax revenue have less incentive to be accountable to their citizens, and resource wealth creates prize worth fighting over — which is correlated with conflict in some contexts. Nigeria is the canonical example: enormous oil wealth alongside persistent poverty, infrastructure deficits, and conflict in the Niger Delta. But the curse is not inevitable: Botswana's diamond revenues have been managed through strong institutions to fund genuine development, and Norway's sovereign wealth fund from oil has been deliberately insulated from short-term political pressures.

commonMistakes: Not knowing the term 'resource curse' or 'Dutch Disease'
Describing only one mechanism without explaining the others
Treating the resource curse as inevitable rather than conditional on governance
followUps: What institutional features seem to make the difference between countries that avoid the curse and those that don't?

question: What is gentrification, and is it a good or bad thing for cities?
lookingFor: Conceptual clarity about what gentrification is, followed by genuinely balanced engagement with the debate — not a one-sided polemic.

bullets: Define gentrification accurately — not just 'an area getting nicer'
Articulate the benefits and the harms with specificity
Arrive at a nuanced position that acknowledges the tension
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Gentrification is the process by which the social and economic character of a lower-income neighbourhood changes as wealthier residents and capital move in — typically accompanied by rising rents, displacement of existing communities, and changes in the commercial landscape. It's become a politically charged term partly because the same process looks very different depending on where you stand: for an incoming resident it looks like neighbourhood improvement; for a long-term resident on a fixed income it looks like exclusion. The case for gentrification is that it can bring investment, improved services, and reduced crime to areas that had experienced disinvestment. The case against is that those benefits often don't reach the people who were already there — displacement pushes them into less well-served areas, concentrating disadvantage rather than addressing it. My own view is that the debate is often misdirected: the problem isn't that neighbourhoods improve, it's that we lack housing policy capable of ensuring existing residents can stay and share in that improvement.

commonMistakes: Defining gentrification vaguely as an area 'becoming nicer'
Taking a strong one-sided position without engaging with the counterargument
Missing the displacement dimension — treating it as purely economic rather than also social and cultural
followUps: What planning or housing policies could allow neighbourhood improvement without displacement?

question: Why are tropical rainforests so biodiverse, and what makes them so fragile when disturbed?
lookingFor: Sound understanding of the ecological conditions that produce high biodiversity and the nutrient-cycling characteristics that make rainforests vulnerable.

bullets: Explain the conditions — stable climate, high productivity, evolutionary time — that drive biodiversity
Describe the closed nutrient cycle and why most nutrients are locked in the biomass, not the soil
Show why this makes forests resilient to natural disturbance but fragile to human clearance
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Tropical rainforests are biodiverse because the conditions — consistently high temperatures, year-round rainfall, intense sunlight — allow continuous high productivity with no cold or dry season to create population bottlenecks. That sustained energy input, over millions of years of relatively stable climate, has allowed an extraordinary radiation of species into highly specialised niches. But the counterintuitive thing is that despite this apparent lushness, the soils underneath are often nutrient-poor. Almost all the nutrients are locked in the biomass itself, cycling very rapidly through decomposition on the forest floor and re-uptake by shallow root systems. When you clear the forest, you expose those thin, laterite soils to intense tropical rain, the nutrients leach away rapidly, and what looked like enormously productive land turns out to support agriculture for only a few years before degrading. The productivity was the forest's — not the land's.

commonMistakes: Assuming rich forest means rich soil — this is one of the most important counterintuitive facts about rainforests
Describing biodiversity without explaining what ecological conditions drive it
Not connecting the nutrient cycle to why clearance is so damaging long-term
followUps: How does this nutrient cycle dynamic affect the economic case for rainforest conservation over agricultural conversion?

question: What do geographers mean by the 'Anthropocene', and is it a useful concept?
lookingFor: Knowledge of the concept and its origins, combined with genuine critical engagement — it's a contested idea and the panel wants to see independent thinking.

bullets: Explain the concept: a proposed geological epoch defined by human impact on Earth systems
Describe what makes it geographically significant — not just a geological label
Engage with the debate: what are its uses and its critics' objections?
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The Anthropocene is a proposed geological epoch in which human activity has become the dominant driver of planetary systems — altering the atmosphere, the ocean's chemistry, the global nitrogen cycle, extinction rates, and even the stratigraphic record. The idea was popularised by Paul Crutzen and Eugene Stoermer in 2000 and has since moved from geology into geography, environmental humanities, and policy discourse. For geographers it's significant because it collapses the traditional boundary between the natural and the human — it says those categories are no longer analytically separable, which challenges disciplines built on that distinction. But it attracts serious critiques. Some argue it glosses over which humans are responsible — calling it the 'Anthropocene' hides the fact that it was built by particular societies, economic systems, and classes, not humanity in general. Andreas Malm and others prefer 'Capitalocene' as more analytically honest. There's also a critique that the framing is so vast it risks paralysis rather than action. I find it useful as a provocation, but I think the political economy critique is right — we need to be specific about agency.

commonMistakes: Treating the Anthropocene as simply a fact rather than a contested proposal
Not engaging with any critique of the concept
Reducing it to 'humans are damaging the environment' without the stratigraphic or systems science dimension
followUps: Does the Anthropocene framing change how you think about conservation — who is responsible for fixing it?

question: Is there a geographer — historical or contemporary — whose work has influenced how you think about the subject?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine engagement with geographical scholarship or thinkers — not just a name drop, but real familiarity with the ideas.

bullets: Name a geographer and briefly explain who they are
Describe a specific idea or work and engage with it substantively
Say what it changed or challenged in your own thinking
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been reading some of David Harvey's work, particularly 'The Condition of Postmodernity'. He's a Marxist geographer who argues that shifts in how capitalism organises time and space have profound effects on culture and identity — what he calls 'time-space compression'. The idea that speeding up communications and transport doesn't just change logistics but actually alters how we experience place and belonging was genuinely new to me. It connected things I'd been thinking about separately — why somewhere like a high street in a small town feels increasingly similar to one in a city, why some communities feel left behind while others feel globally connected — and gave me a theoretical frame for both. I don't agree with everything Harvey argues, but he's made me much more interested in the relationship between economic geography and cultural geography.

commonMistakes: Naming a very famous figure like Darwin or Humboldt without showing any familiarity with their actual geographical ideas
Summarising the work without saying what it meant to you personally
Choosing a geographer whose work is unrelated to any of your stated interests
followUps: What would you push back on in their argument?
